posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
My single-blind group met last night to sample 2011 Barolo. By and large this confirmed the reputation of the vintage as relatively open and soft. Not a vintage of great depth, based on these wines. The best are pleasant now, but lacked concentration and grip. The relatively high alcohols did not stand out. (Most were marked at 14.5%, one at 15%.)

They were poured into serving bottles about 90 minutes ahead of the tasting to preserve anonymity, then drunk over another 90 minutes. People were told what the wines were but not the order. (I knew which were the Brovias because they arrived late. Most others did not know.) These were served with food — pretty essential when tasting young wines this tannic, I believe.

Ranked in descending order by my ranking.

Brovia - Villero (Castiglione) ($68): Tight on the nose at first, then some red fruits and floral notes. Big — lots of power and depth. Backward and structured relative to the others, with better concentration. The standout to me. 91+ points. Group #1, my #1.

Fenocchio - Villero (Castiglione) ($43): Nice earthy nebbiolo scents, with a hint of cinnamon on the nose. Medium bodied, quite drinkable. More earthy than fruity. Seems to gain grip with air, though that might just be the effect of eight tannic young Barolos on my palate. 90+. Group #7/8, my #2. Interesting that my top two picks were both from Villero, and my #3 was from Ravera. All three are at relatively altitudes, for what it’s worth.

Elvio Cogno - Ravera (Novello) ($55): Tight at first, more earthy than fruity. Slight dirty dishrag on the nose for a few moments (dirty glass?). Good grip and more concentration than some. More masculine, with significant power. This is slower to open than most others. 89 points for me, though I think that might be a bit low. Group #4, my #3.

Brovia - Ca Mia/Brea (Serralunga) ($68): This was opened and decanted just before we started, and it was more closed than the others initially. Perhaps that was the late opening; perhaps that’s because it’s from Serralunga. Darker in color. Something oddly vegetal came up on the nose and in the mouth. Elegant, maybe a tad low on acid and a little lacking in concentration, like most of these wines. I gave it 87 and then bumped that up to 91 at the end. Group #5/6, my #4 based on the initial points.

Burlotto - Cannubi (Barolo) ($73): Tight on the nose, not giving a lot initially. In the mouth, this is soft, luscious and ripe. Juniper flavors more than classic nebbiolo elements. “I’m looking for some tannin,” I wrote. Not a lot of grip. Marked at 15%, the only one market at more than 14.5%. The alcohol doesn’t stand out, but this is ripe and round. 87 at first, but I marked this up as it sat out and I topped up the glass. 91 at the end. Group #3; my #5 based on original score.

Giuseppe Rinaldi - Brunate (Barolo/La Morra) ($130): Spicy, cinnamon on the nose, with ripe, sweet scents. In the mouth, sweet and spicy. Very ripe, and the fruit became increasingly candied as it sat out. Someone said it reminded them of New World pinot. I didn’t find it cloying, but it was certainly atypical. The candied/cough drop flavors carried into the finish. Overall, I found this kind of disjointed. 86. Group #5/6, my #6.
According to Italian Wine Merchant, “Each bottle of Barolo is also given more breadth and acidity through a ripasso in which Barbera skins are added to the Nebbiolo must, a practice that Giuseppe explains is (and always has been) common but which is never spoken of today.” Is that really right? Perhaps that explains the odd fruit profile. 86? Group #5/6, my #6

Elio Grasso - Gavarini Vigna Chiniera (Monforte) ($60): Lovely classic nebbiolo scents at first, then new oak started to show. More and more oak emerged as the wine sat in the glass and was topped up. Medium body, quite drinkable now, but not much grip. The oak began to show in the mouth, too, over time, so eventually the wine was completely dominated by new oak. They claim this is aged in 2,500 hl casks but I’d say that must be baloney. Fairly hard tannins on the back. 85- for me. Group #2, my #7. (This group tends to rate wines high when they have new oak.)

Marcarini - Brunate (Barolo/La Morra) ($57.50): Lighter color than most (as is typical for Marcarini). Floral, rose hip scents. More tannin than some but only medium bodied — not a powerhouse. “A tad thin,” I wrote. Seems very La Morra-ish. A trace of caramel and a faint hint of oxidation at the back of the palate and in the finish, which was odd. A bad cork, perhaps? 84, but that’s probably ungenerous. Group #8, my #8.
Note: I find Marcarini’s wines often show as light, even dilute, early on.

posts.name: Tony Carucci
posts.cooked: I’ve had most of these wines and the Fenocchio Villero was a standout for me in 2011. The Giuseppe Rinaldi failed to impress.

posts.name: Daniel Mattisson
posts.cooked: I could be wrong, but from my visit at Grasso I don’t believe the Chiniera sees any new oak, it is aged in botti. Only their riserva sees barrique.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Daniel Mattisson:

I could be wrong, but from my visit at Grasso I don’t believe the Chiniera sees any new oak, it is aged in botti. Only their riserva sees barrique.

Wink wink, nudge nudge.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Daniel Mattisson:

I could be wrong, but from my visit at Grasso I don’t believe the Chiniera sees any new oak, it is aged in botti. Only their riserva sees barrique.

That’s what they say on the website, but this had oodles of new oak. (The Runcot Barolo also is aged in barriques.)

When I visited in 2004, they had a lot of barriques in the cellar. I wondered then if it wasn’t more than they needed for barbera and the Runcot.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
John,

Good notes

We had the similar experience with the 2010 Grasso (and oak) which we opened as part of our 2010 tasting that I posted few months ago.

I too find Marcarini too ‘light’ when tasted at release. Not tasted older versions too see if they put an weight and flesh out.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Tony C:

I’ve had most of these wines and the Fenocchio Villero was a standout for me in 2011. The Giuseppe Rinaldi failed to impress.

Any idea how this compares with the 2010?

And not directed to you, but to the group: there was an earlier note praising Rinaldi’s Tre Tine from 2011. Do you believe there is such a gap between this and the Brunate this year that one would show so poorly and the other so well?

posts.name: Tony Carucci
posts.cooked:
Markus S:
Tony C:

I’ve had most of these wines and the Fenocchio Villero was a standout for me in 2011. The Giuseppe Rinaldi failed to impress.

Any idea how this compares with the 2010?

And not directed to you, but to the group: there was an earlier note praising Rinaldi’s Tre Tine from 2011. Do you believe there is such a gap between this and the Brunate this year that one would show so poorly and the other so well?

Unfortunately, Fenocchio was one producer I did not try in 2010. I also had the Tre Tine and thought it was more supple, not as focused and lacked depth.
Bill K praised the 2011 Tre Tine.

posts.name: Tvrtko Cernos
posts.cooked:
Tony C:

Bill K praised the 2011 Tre Tine.

He may well have, but I think the gist of his case, as I read it, is that we cannot really know how good exactly it is before some time around 2055.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Markus S:
Tony C:

I’ve had most of these wines and the Fenocchio Villero was a standout for me in 2011. The Giuseppe Rinaldi failed to impress.

Any idea how this compares with the 2010?

And not directed to you, but to the group: there was an earlier note praising Rinaldi’s Tre Tine from 2011. Do you believe there is such a gap between this and the Brunate this year that one would show so poorly and the other so well?

Its not fair to call this a poor wine, it was an extreme wine. Some folks in the group loved it. It had a lot of red fruit, but it was sweet/medicinal fruit. Some folks (I’d count myself in that number) can’t stand that profile.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Your group was awful tough on that Fenocchio. No one else seems to agree with those yahoo’s. Anyhow, I’ll open one soon to see how much cinnamon and spice there is.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
For what it’s worth, I’ve enjoyed the G Rinaldi Brunate quite a bit. We had a bottle with an 8-hour decant or so back in June, which took on quite a dark and balsamic fruit profile after all that air. I could imagine it bearing some similarity to the 2000 in due course.

I also quite liked the Burlotto Cannubi, though the beautifully aromatic and singular Monvigliero is where I put my money.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Robert Pavlovich:

Your group was awful tough on that Fenocchio. No one else seems to agree with those yahoo’s. Anyhow, I’ll open one soon to see how much cinnamon and spice there is.

The rankings were pretty randomly distributed last night, as they often are where there aren’t any defective wines and nothing that’s off-the-charts good.

Gray Newman ranked the Fennocchio 3rd, and our palates are typically pretty close. On the other hand, two other people whose tastes are pretty close to mine ranked it 8th. David Zylberberg ranked it 6th and Corey Miller ranked it 5th. You get the idea.

Even the #1 Brovia Villero had two 7th place votes from people I tend to agree with. It was just one of those nights – no clear favorites or dogs.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked: Remarkably sweeping conclusions about the vintage being drawn from a tiny sampling of wines here, gentlemen. Among other things, there are a number of better 2011s out there that are not tepresented in your tasting, and 25% of your sample comes from a single winemaker. I am not trying to rain on your parade, nor call into question what you experienced, individually or collectively, but I do not think that you can legimately extrapolate the results here much beyond the wines tasted…

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Speaking to the Fenocchio specifically, it’s received praise well above its weight in the early going, including prior CT notes. But now we’ll have multiple notes up based on 1 bottle. Small samples indeed, though they have a bigger impact in the early going, which is just one reason why folks are keen to get TN’s up early.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked: Who the hell cares about getting notes up early? That’s the oddest thing I’ve ever heard.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
John Morris:
Daniel Mattisson:

I could be wrong, but from my visit at Grasso I don’t believe the Chiniera sees any new oak, it is aged in botti. Only their riserva sees barrique.

That’s what they say on the website, but this had oodles of new oak. (The Runcot Barolo also is aged in barriques.)

When I visited in 2004, they had a lot of barriques in the cellar. I wondered then if it wasn’t more than they needed for barbera and the Runcot.

Thanks for the notes. One question, why do you think the winery would mis-represent their oak treatment? During our recent visit, we were told the Runcot Riserva is the only Barolo to see barrique. The other two Barolos – Casa Matte and Chiera – are aged in botti. Our tour seemed to confirm that but I took them at their word.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
My notes below. I generally thought these were very nice wines, if perhaps a bit lacking in oomph. My favorites were the more powerful bottles, whereas normally I like more elegant Nebbiolo.

John Morris:

My single-blind group met last night to sample 2011 Barolo. By and large this confirmed the reputation of the vintage as relatively open and soft. Not a vintage of great depth, based on these wines. The best are pleasant now, but lacked concentration and grip. The relatively high alcohols did not stand out.



Brovia - Villero (Castiglione) ($68):Tight on the nose at first, then some red fruits and floral notes. Big — lots of power and depth. Backward and structured relative to the others, with better concentration. The standout to me. 91+ points. Group #1, my #1.

My 2nd place wine. Somewhat closed nose - red fruit, coriander. Austere, with biggest tannin by far, but also a great, savory/saline character and the sense of a lot of fruit lurking. I like this lots.

[b

]Fenocchio - Villero (Castiglione) ($43): [/b]Nice earthy nebbiolo scents, with a hint of cinnamon on the nose. Medium bodied, quite drinkable. More earthy than fruity. Seems to gain grip with air, though that might just be the effect of eight tannic young Barolos on my palate. 90+. Group #7/8, my #2. Interesting that my top two picks were both from Villero, and my #3 was from Ravera. All three are at relatively altitudes, for what it’s worth.

Most of the group thought there was something funny going on in the nose here- but we couldn’t agree on what it was. For me, this smelled strongly, distractingly, of cucumber. Some red fruits and plenty of heat on the finish. Surprisingly accessible.

Elvio Cogno - Ravera (Novello) ($55): > Tight at first, more earthy than fruity. Slight dirty dishrag on the nose for a few moments (dirty glass?). Good grip and more concentration than some. More masculine, with significant power. This is slower to open than most others. 89 points for me, though I think that might be a bit low. Group #4, my #3.

My 1st. So savory/earthy. Ginger and anchovies add some complexity to the by now familiar red fruit / roses profile. In the mouth I wrote “salty!”, which is a compliment. Lots of powdery tannin. Coiled up, dense, but very promising.

Brovia - Ca Mia/Brea (Serralunga) ($68): > This was opened and decanted just before we started, and it was more closed than the others initially. Perhaps that was the late opening; perhaps that’s because it’s from Serralunga. Darker in color. Something oddly vegetal came up on the nose and in the mouth. Elegant, maybe a tad low on acid and a little lacking in concentration, like most of these wines. I gave it 87 and then bumped that up to 91 at the end. Group #5/6, my #4 based on the initial points.

My 3rd. Clean, ripe, and open. Lots of roses, almost stemmy like a whole cluster Pinot, lots of red fruit, ripe, rich, lower in acid than the others but it gives this a certain lushness, an easy wine to like but not at all unbalanced. Yum.

Burlotto - Cannubi (Barolo) ($73): > Tight on the nose, not giving a lot initially. In the mouth, this is soft, luscious and ripe. Juniper flavors more than classic nebbiolo elements. “I’m looking for some tannin,” I wrote. Not a lot of grip. Marked at 15%, the only one market at more than 14.5%. The alcohol doesn’t stand out, but this is ripe and round. 87 at first, but I marked this up as it sat out and I topped up the glass. 91 at the end. Group #3; my #5 based on original score.

This shows its heat to me - I wrote down for the nose: “spirity, juniper. A Gin and tonic?” But this is fairly straightforward once past the nose, if somewhat undistinguished. My 5th.

[

b]Giuseppe Rinaldi - Brunate (Barolo/La Morra) ($130): [/b]Spicy, cinnamon on the nose, with ripe, sweet scents. In the mouth, sweet and spicy. Very ripe, and the fruit became increasingly candied as it sat out. Someone said it reminded them of New World pinot. I didn’t find it cloying, but it was certainly atypical. The candied/cough drop flavors carried into the finish. Overall, I found this kind of disjointed. 86. Group #5/6, my #6.
According to Italian Wine Merchant, “Each bottle of Barolo is also given more breadth and acidity through a ripasso in which Barbera skins are added to the Nebbiolo must, a practice that Giuseppe explains is (and always has been) common but which is never spoken of today.” Is that really right? Perhaps that explains the odd fruit profile. 86? Group #5/6, my #6

My notes for this have “candied” three times, “medicinal” twice, and “I don’t like this” scrawled in big letters. Yuck. Robitussin. My 8th.

Elio Grasso - Gavarini Vigna Chiniera (Monforte) ($60): > Lovely classic nebbiolo scents at first, then new oak started to show. More and more oak emerged as the wine sat in the glass and was topped up. Medium body, quite drinkable now, but not much grip. The oak began to show in the mouth, too, over time, so eventually the wine was completely dominated by new oak. They claim this is aged in 2,500 hl casks but I’d say that must be baloney. Fairly hard tannins on the back. 85- for me. Group #2, my #7. (This group tends to rate wines high when they have new oak.)

I wrote “oak? What’s with all the caramel?” Lots of heat. Lots of oak. And red fruit and flowers, but the oak sticks out here. The knowledgeable folks were all over this as the Grasso blind, and discussion of how full of it he is ensued. My 7th.

Marcarini - Brunate (Barolo/La Morra) ($57.50): > Lighter color than most (as is typical for Marcarini). Floral, rose hip scents. More tannin than some but only medium bodied — not a powerhouse. “A tad thin,” I wrote. Seems very La Morra-ish. A trace of caramel and a faint hint of oxidation at the back of the palate and in the finish, which was odd. A bad cork, perhaps? 84, but that’s probably ungenerous. Group #8, my #8.
Note: I find Marcarini’s wines often show as light, even dilute, early on.

I liked this more than John. There was a hint of something oxidative here but I’d don’t mind a touch of ginger in my reds. Certainly the darkest and tarriest of tonight’s wines, though not showing ripeness in other ways, as it’s only medium bodied and quite savory. To me this was a bit more classic than the others which were strongly to the red fruit / roses side of the Nebbiolo spectrum. My 4th.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
DavidZ:

Who the hell cares about getting notes up early? That’s the oddest thing I’ve ever heard.

No one has yet to admit to it (that I know of), but it’s most certainly a thing.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Robert Pavlovich:
DavidZ:

Who the hell cares about getting notes up early? That’s the oddest thing I’ve ever heard.

No one has yet to admit to it (that I know of), but it’s most certainly a thing.

But why would it be? I don’t understand why there’s any incentive.
